title: GoalOS AGIALPHA Ascension — Sovereign Machine Economy ✨
tagline: AI creates output. GoalOS creates proof.
subtitle: An institutional-grade operating surface for proof-settled autonomous AI work, governed decision states, validator-gated jobs, and reusable capability packages.

generated_from.name: GoalOS AGIALPHA Ascension
generated_from.role: upstream evidence-first GoalOS repository and claim-boundary source

operating_law: No Evidence Docket, no strong public claim.
claim_boundary: This repository is a research, product, and governance scaffold. It does not claim achieved AGI, ASI, superintelligence, empirical SOTA, guaranteed security, guaranteed returns, legal approval, tax approval, production authorization, user-fund authorization, autonomous legal sovereignty, mainnet activation, or civilization-scale capability.

kpis.label: Proof surfaces
kpis.detail: schemas and examples for evidence-bearing work

kpis.label: Generated pages
kpis.detail: public website pages generated from manifest and evidence standards

kpis.label: Operator controls

kpis.label: Launch path
kpis.detail: paste one workflow into GitHub Web UI

kpis.label: Evidence room

kpis.label: Scale claim gate

kpis.label: Proof velocity

quick_launch: Create a public GitHub repository named goalos-agialpha-sovereign-machine-economy.
Set Actions workflow permissions to Read and write permissions.
Set Pages source to GitHub Actions.
Paste the autonomous workflow and commit.
Run the workflow from the Actions tab.
Copy the Pages URL into the repository About box.

pillars.title: Mission OS
pillars.text: Converts objectives into bounded Mission Contracts and Governed Decision States.

pillars.title: Meta-Agentic Council
pillars.text: Creates, selects, evaluates, reconfigures, and retires agents under proof gates.

pillars.title: AGI Jobs Ledger
pillars.text: Turns autonomous work into constrained proof jobs with evidence requirements.

pillars.title: Node Runtime
pillars.text: Keeps operators in control with pause, rollback, identity, validator, and observability surfaces.

pillars.title: Evidence Dockets
pillars.text: Bundle sources, traces, contradiction registers, risks, costs, validator reports, and claims matrices.

pillars.title: Trust Center
pillars.text: Publishes the non-sensitive proof surface, standards, QA reports, and claim boundary.

pages.title: Home
pages.headline: Autonomous AI work, made evidence-bearing.
pages.body: GoalOS AGIALPHA Ascension turns ambitious autonomous AI work into bounded missions, evidence dockets, validator reports, release gates, settlement receipts, and reusable capability packages.

sections.title: The executive thesis
sections.text: The next practical frontier is not merely more agents. It is governed, observable, proof-bearing work that an institution can review, reproduce, settle, and reuse.

cards.title: Output is not enough
cards.text: A result becomes institutionally useful only when it carries evidence, traceability, risk accounting, and validation.

cards.title: Capability must compound
cards.text: Accepted work is captured as Chronicle entries and reusable capability packages rather than isolated chat outputs.

cards.title: Publication is gated
cards.text: The public website is generated from proof-aligned source and always carries the claim boundary.

sections.title: One-file public launch
sections.text: A non-technical operator can launch the repository from GitHub Web UI by pasting one workflow file and pressing Run workflow.
checklist: Create a public GitHub repository named goalos-agialpha-sovereign-machine-economy.
Set Actions workflow permissions to Read and write permissions.
Set Pages source to GitHub Actions.
Paste the autonomous workflow and commit.
Run the workflow from the Actions tab.
Copy the Pages URL into the repository About box.

sections.title: Repository promise
sections.text: Corporate polish, research discipline, public trust posture, machine-readable artifacts, and a clear path from demo to pilot without overstating evidence.

pages.title: Start Here
pages.headline: Create the repository from GitHub.com in minutes.
pages.body: No terminal is required. This page is the practical path for creating the repository, running the autonomous action, and publishing the website.

sections.title: Before you begin
sections.text: You need a GitHub account and permission to create a new repository. Public visibility is easiest for GitHub Pages.

sections.title: Click-by-click path
sections.text: Create the repository, set Actions to read/write, set Pages to GitHub Actions, paste the workflow, commit it, then run it.
checklist: Create a public GitHub repository named goalos-agialpha-sovereign-machine-economy.
Set Actions workflow permissions to Read and write permissions.
Set Pages source to GitHub Actions.
Paste the autonomous workflow and commit.
Run the workflow from the Actions tab.
Copy the Pages URL into the repository About box.

sections.title: After the run succeeds
sections.text: Open reports/site-qa.json, reports/repo-validation.json, reports/claim-scan.json, and the live Pages URL. Add the URL to the repository About section.

pages.title: Mission OS
pages.headline: From objective to governed decision state.
pages.body: Mission OS converts a high-stakes objective into a bounded contract, evidence plan, validation process, decision state, and reusable capability.

sections.title: Mission lifecycle
sections.text: Every mission starts with a bounded objective and ends with a reviewable evidence package, not a vague autonomous action.

cards.title: Mission Contract
cards.text: Defines scope, success criteria, constraints, non-goals, validation requirements, and human review gates.

cards.title: Claims Matrix
cards.text: Separates facts, inferences, recommendations, assumptions, and unsupported claims.

cards.title: Governed Decision State
cards.text: Records whether the work is accepted, rejected, blocked, deferred, or ready for limited release.

sections.title: Practical value
sections.text: This lets operators explain what the system did, what it relied on, what risks were controlled, and what still needs review.

pages.title: Capability Stack
pages.headline: A complete operating model for proof-settled AI work.
pages.body: The repository is structured as a stack: user-facing launch experience, mission engine, proof jobs, evidence layer, validation layer, runtime controls, governance, and publication.

sections.title: Stack layers
sections.text: Each layer has a purpose, artifact, and review gate.

cards.title: Operator UX
cards.text: Plain-language launch guides, website editor guide, and non-technical troubleshooting.

cards.title: Proof Kernel
cards.text: Minimal reference logic for DONE gating, settlement readiness, and review status.

cards.title: Trust Center
cards.text: Public artifacts, claim boundaries, QA reports, standards, and release-state information.

sections.title: Why this matters
sections.text: The repository can be inspected by executives, developers, reviewers, operators, and governance stakeholders without requiring one audience to read everything.

pages.title: Agent Foundry
pages.headline: Agents that manage agents remain governed by proof.
pages.body: The META-AGENTIC α‑AGI lineage is reimplemented as an Agent Foundry that creates, selects, evaluates, reconfigures, and retires agents under explicit governance.

sections.title: Agent operating rules
sections.text: Agents are not treated as free-floating personalities. They are accountable work units with roles, permissions, evidence duties, and retirement criteria.

cards.title: Create
cards.text: Spawn candidate agents only when a mission contract justifies the role.

cards.title: Evaluate
cards.text: Compare agents using task evidence, validator reports, and delayed outcomes.

cards.title: Retire or reconfigure
cards.text: Remove or adapt agents when evidence quality, safety, or cost profiles degrade.

sections.title: Institutional guardrail
sections.text: No agent output is promoted to capability without an Evidence Docket and validation status.

pages.title: AGI Jobs Ledger
pages.headline: Bounded work packets with evidence obligations.
pages.body: The AGI Jobs layer turns missions into proof jobs: concrete units of work with owners, tools, traces, acceptance criteria, and settlement requirements.

sections.title: Job design
sections.text: A useful job is small enough to verify and rich enough to produce meaningful work.

cards.title: Bounded scope
cards.text: Each job states its objective, constraints, non-goals, and expected output.

cards.title: Trace requirements
cards.text: Tool actions, inputs, outputs, timestamps, costs, and provenance are recorded.

cards.title: Acceptance gate
cards.text: Settlement is blocked until validation and review requirements are met.

sections.title: Operator benefit
sections.text: The ledger gives non-technical reviewers a clean way to see what happened and why it passed or failed.

pages.title: Node Runtime
pages.headline: Operator control for autonomous work.
pages.body: The Node Runtime maps AGI Alpha Node v0 into a GoalOS operator plane: identity, validator gating, pause/resume, telemetry, release state, and rollback authority.

sections.title: Control surfaces
sections.text: The runtime is designed so operators can stop, review, redirect, or roll back work when the evidence or risk posture requires it.

cards.title: Pause and resume
cards.text: Stop work when validation fails, risk rises, or policy changes.

cards.title: Validator roster
cards.text: Track who or what can validate work and under which criteria.

cards.title: Observability
cards.text: Expose mission status, job status, docket completeness, risk ledgers, and release state.

sections.title: Boundary
sections.text: Runtime control is architectural in this repository and does not imply production authorization or user-fund authorization.

pages.title: Evidence Layer
pages.headline: Proof that can be reviewed, reproduced, and challenged.
pages.body: Evidence Dockets are the core institutional unit: claims, source provenance, contradiction registers, tool traces, verifier reports, costs, risks, and outcomes.

sections.title: Docket contents
sections.text: An Evidence Docket should make it easy to understand the work without trusting the agent blindly.

cards.title: Claims Matrix
cards.text: Every important claim is categorized and linked to supporting evidence.

cards.title: Source Provenance
cards.text: Sources, files, tools, and generated artifacts are recorded with stable references.

cards.title: Validator Reports
cards.text: Review results state what passed, what failed, what is unresolved, and what requires human approval.

sections.title: Institutional standard
sections.text: No strong public claim should be published unless a docket can carry it.

pages.title: Governance
pages.headline: The system remains ambitious because it remains bounded.
pages.body: Governance turns proof into controlled decisions: accept, reject, block, defer, request more evidence, release internally, or publish externally.

sections.title: Governance mode
sections.text: The repository favors boringly clear decision records over vague autonomous behavior.

cards.title: Release gates
cards.text: Public claims, website changes, standards updates, and capability propagation require checks.

cards.title: Risk ledger
cards.text: Known risks are recorded with severity, owner, mitigation, and status.

cards.title: Rollback plan
cards.text: Each release has a defined recovery path when evidence, claims, or site QA fail.

sections.title: Practical outcome
sections.text: The repo is easier to trust because its claims are intentionally constrained.

pages.title: Trust Center
pages.headline: A public proof surface for serious reviewers.
pages.body: The Trust Center organizes the public proof, standards, claim boundary, QA reports, and operator guidance that make the repository credible.

sections.title: Trust center surfaces
sections.text: Visitors should be able to find what the system claims, what it does not claim, what artifacts exist, and how validation works.

cards.title: Reports
cards.text: Site QA, claim scan, artifact digests, repository validation, and scorecard outputs.

cards.title: Standards
cards.text: GoalOS standards for mission contracts, proof jobs, evidence dockets, validator reports, release gates, and scorecards.

cards.title: Privacy posture
cards.text: Public proof, private data: publish references and review status without exposing confidential inputs.

sections.title: Corporate readiness
sections.text: The Trust Center is designed for procurement, board review, partner diligence, and community review.

pages.title: Operator Console Blueprint
pages.headline: A clear cockpit for mission control.
pages.body: The operator experience is built around visible mission status, evidence completeness, validation state, risk posture, and publication readiness.

sections.title: Operator panels
sections.text: The intended product experience is a cockpit, not a chat window.

cards.title: Mission board
cards.text: Shows active missions, current gates, owners, due dates, and blocked items.

cards.title: Evidence cockpit
cards.text: Shows what proof exists, what is missing, and what validators said.

cards.title: Release console
cards.text: Shows whether a release is ready, blocked, or safe for limited publication.

sections.title: Non-technical UX rule
sections.text: Every important status should be readable without opening code.

pages.title: Pilot Program
pages.headline: Start with bounded missions that prove the workflow.
pages.body: The recommended first deployment is not open-ended autonomy. It is a controlled pilot with bounded missions, clear baselines, validation, and evidence review.

sections.title: Pilot design
sections.text: Pick work where proof matters and outcomes can be judged.

cards.title: Mission 1: Research brief
cards.text: Convert a research question into a claims matrix, source provenance, and executive brief.

cards.title: Mission 2: Vendor diligence
cards.text: Create a docket comparing vendor claims, risks, costs, and contradictions.

cards.title: Mission 3: Release readiness
cards.text: Run a release gate over website copy, claims, docs, and QA artifacts.

sections.title: Success criteria
sections.text: The pilot succeeds when the review process is faster, clearer, safer, and more reusable than ad hoc AI work.

pages.title: Enterprise Readiness
pages.headline: Built for institutional review, not just developer novelty.
pages.body: The repository includes procurement, compliance, risk, board, data-room, and operating-metric surfaces so serious organizations can evaluate it.

sections.title: Enterprise artifacts
sections.text: A technical repo becomes enterprise-readable when it includes governance, operations, assurance, and rollout materials.

cards.title: Procurement readiness
cards.text: Explains scope, boundaries, dependencies, data handling, and support expectations.

cards.title: Model risk management
cards.text: Frames AI outputs as evidence-gated work products, not automatic truth.

cards.title: Board-level brief
cards.text: Gives leadership a practical understanding of the opportunity and boundaries.

sections.title: Practical posture
sections.text: This is a launch-grade public package; production deployment still requires implementation review, security review, and authorized operating procedures.

pages.title: Standards
pages.headline: A shared language for proof-settled work.
pages.body: The standards directory defines the artifact contracts and review expectations that keep missions auditable and interoperable.

sections.title: Standards included
sections.text: The repository ships with twelve GoalOS standards covering mission contracts, proof jobs, tool traces, evidence dockets, validator reports, Chronicle entries, settlement receipts, agent cards, publication dockets, release gates, and scorecards.

sections.title: Why standards matter
sections.text: Reusable capability requires stable forms. Standards let different agents, validators, and operators understand the same proof objects.

pages.title: Schema Registry
pages.headline: Proof artifacts that software can validate.
pages.body: JSON schemas make the proof surface machine-readable, testable, and automation-friendly.

sections.title: Schema role
sections.text: Schemas do not make claims true. They make proof artifacts structured enough to validate, compare, index, and review.

sections.title: Included schemas
sections.text: Mission contracts, proof jobs, tool traces, evidence dockets, validator reports, risk ledgers, settlement receipts, capability packages, Chronicle entries, and more.

pages.title: Evaluation
pages.headline: Evidence before stronger claims.
pages.body: The evaluation program defines how missions are compared against baselines, reviewed by validators, tested with delayed outcomes, and documented for reproduction.

sections.title: Evaluation requirements
sections.text: A strong empirical claim needs task definition, baseline, proof bundle, replay or reproduction path, validator report, cost ledger, risk ledger, and delayed-outcome review.

sections.title: Claim discipline
sections.text: Without an Evidence Docket, the system should not make a strong empirical claim.

pages.title: Proof Economy
pages.headline: Verified work becomes reusable capability.
pages.body: The Sovereign Machine Economy is modeled as a proof-settled loop: work only becomes reusable economic capacity after evidence and validation.

sections.title: Economic loop
sections.text: Missions produce proof jobs; proof jobs produce dockets; accepted dockets produce settlement receipts; settled work becomes Chronicle entries and capability packages.

sections.title: Boundary
sections.text: This is an architectural model, not a financial return claim, investment offering, or production settlement system.

pages.title: Release Gates
pages.headline: Do not publish what the evidence cannot carry.
pages.body: Release gates protect the public repository by checking site quality, claim boundaries, schema validity, proof reports, and rollback readiness.

sections.title: Gate checks
sections.text: The action runs site QA, claim scan, repository validation, artifact hashing, scorecard computation, demo mission, and unit tests before deployment.

sections.title: Rollback expectation
sections.text: A release without a rollback path should not be treated as complete.

pages.title: Security
pages.headline: Safety through limits, review, and transparent boundaries.
pages.body: The repository avoids implying production security. It provides a public security posture, responsible disclosure process, secrets guidance, and review expectations.

sections.title: Security principles
sections.text: Least privilege, no secrets in public proof artifacts, bounded claims, review gates, artifact digests, and human authorization for sensitive transitions.

sections.title: Boundary
sections.text: The repository is not a completed external audit and should not be represented as one.

pages.title: Roadmap
pages.headline: A practical path from public scaffold to serious pilot.
pages.body: The roadmap moves from repository launch to pilot mission packs, validator integrations, proof APIs, operator console, trust center, and production hardening.

sections.title: Phases
sections.text: Launch the public proof surface, run controlled pilots, expand validation, implement APIs, add operator UI, then pursue independent review.

sections.title: Review gate
sections.text: Each roadmap step needs evidence of usefulness, risk control, cost, and reproducibility before stronger claims.

pages.title: Source Lineage
pages.headline: One GoalOS-native surface for prior lineages.
pages.body: The repository integrates the previous META-AGENTIC α‑AGI, AGI Alpha Node, and AGI Jobs lineages under the GoalOS proof discipline.

sections.title: Integrated lineages
sections.text: Agent creation and evaluation become the Agent Foundry; node owner controls become the Node Runtime; jobs and CI discipline become the AGI Jobs Ledger; Mission OS supplies the proof-to-action architecture.

sections.title: Respecting boundaries
sections.text: The repo preserves a strict distinction between architecture, demonstration, evidence, and production authorization.

pages.title: Repository Map
pages.headline: Know where everything lives.
pages.body: The repository is organized so non-technical operators, developers, reviewers, and executives can find their path quickly.

sections.title: Primary directories
sections.text: content for website source, docs for operating manuals, standards for artifact rules, schemas for contracts, examples for proof objects, scripts for automation, public for generated website, and reports for QA outputs.

sections.title: First files to read
sections.text: START_HERE.md, README.md, CLAIMS.md, docs/NON_TECHNICAL_OPERATOR_MANUAL.md, docs/EXECUTIVE_INSTITUTIONAL_BRIEF.md, and reports/site-qa.json.

pages.title: Launch Package
pages.headline: A polished public release surface.
pages.body: The launch package includes repository About copy, topics, website copy, demo script, stakeholder guide, and a non-technical GitHub UI path.

sections.title: Public copy
sections.text: GoalOS turns autonomous AI work into evidence-bearing institutional capability.

sections.title: Recommended topics
sections.text: goalos, agialpha, sovereign-machine-economy, autonomous-ai, evidence-docket, proof-bundles, validator-mesh, governed-ai, machine-labor, institutional-ai.

pages.title: FAQ
pages.headline: Straight answers for non-technical reviewers.
pages.body: This FAQ explains what the repository is, what the action does, what the website is, and what the project does not claim.

sections.title: Is this production software?
sections.text: No. It is a launch-grade repository, proof architecture, product scaffold, standards package, and website generator. Production use requires further implementation and review.

sections.title: Will the action change my repository?
sections.text: Yes, in direct commit mode. It creates files, runs QA, commits generated artifacts, and publishes Pages. Use dry run or pull request mode for a safer review path.

pages.title: Executive Brief
pages.headline: The boardroom explanation in one page.
pages.body: GoalOS is a disciplined way to convert AI output into reviewable work, reviewable work into trusted decisions, and trusted decisions into reusable capability.

sections.title: Why now
sections.text: Organizations are adopting agentic systems faster than their evidence, governance, and audit practices can mature. GoalOS makes proof the operating primitive.

sections.title: What leadership should ask
sections.text: What was done? What evidence supports it? What did it cost? What risks remain? Who validated it? Can it be reused?

pages.title: Claim Boundary
pages.headline: Ambition without unsupported claims.
pages.body: This repository is a research, product, and governance scaffold. It does not claim achieved AGI, ASI, superintelligence, empirical SOTA, guaranteed security, guaranteed returns, legal approval, tax approval, production authorization, user-fund authorization, autonomous legal sovereignty, mainnet activation, or civilization-scale capability.

sections.title: What this repository does not claim
sections.text: It does not claim achieved AGI, ASI, empirical SOTA, production authorization, external audit completion, guaranteed returns, legal approval, tax approval, user-fund authorization, or civilization-scale capability.

sections.title: What would justify stronger claims
sections.text: Real tasks, baselines, Evidence Dockets, replay logs, validator reports, cost and safety ledgers, delayed outcomes, and independent review.

pages.title: Adoption Playbook
pages.headline: How a serious organization would start.
pages.body: Adoption should begin with bounded, low-risk proof missions and expand only after evidence quality, operator experience, and review practices are strong.

sections.title: First 30 days
sections.text: Launch the repository, run a proof mission, review dockets, tune schemas, define validators, and create a pilot scorecard.

sections.title: Next 60 days
sections.text: Add domain-specific mission templates, verifier rosters, operator dashboards, and a private evidence store.

pages.title: Data Room
pages.headline: Diligence-ready public materials.
pages.body: The repository includes a data-room index for reviewers who need fast access to architecture, governance, security, claim boundaries, QA reports, standards, and pilot design.

sections.title: Diligence package
sections.text: Executive brief, trust center, standards registry, security posture, claim boundary, release gates, repository validation, and artifact digest reports.

sections.title: Private extension
sections.text: In a real deployment, sensitive inputs, customer data, secrets, credentials, and private validator notes stay out of public Pages.

pages.title: Operating Metrics
pages.headline: Measure proof quality, not just activity.
pages.body: GoalOS metrics focus on mission completion quality: evidence completeness, validation pass rate, contradiction resolution, cost, latency, rollback frequency, and capability reuse.

sections.title: Useful metrics
sections.text: Docket completeness, validation latency, rejected claim rate, unresolved risk count, replay success, cost per accepted mission, and capability reuse count.

sections.title: Avoid vanity metrics
sections.text: Agent count, message count, and token volume are not proof of institutional value.

pages.title: Website Autopilot
pages.headline: A public site generated from proof-aligned source.
pages.body: The website is generated from content/site_manifest.json, checked by automation, and deployed through GitHub Pages.

sections.title: Edit without code
sections.text: Change content/site_manifest.json, rerun the workflow, and the public pages regenerate.

sections.title: Publication guardrails
sections.text: The generated site includes the claim boundary, status JSON, search index, sitemap, and QA checks.

pages.title: Troubleshooting
pages.headline: What to do when GitHub does not behave as expected.
pages.body: Most launch issues are settings issues: Pages source, workflow permissions, missing workflow_dispatch, or a typo in the workflow file path.

sections.title: Common fixes
sections.text: Check that the file path is exactly .github/workflows/goalos-ascension-autopilot.yml, Actions are enabled, workflow permissions are read/write, and Pages source is GitHub Actions.

sections.title: If the website is not live yet
sections.text: Open Actions, wait for the deploy job to finish, then open Settings → Pages for the final URL.

pages.title: Evidence to Scale
pages.headline: Institutional ambition, tested by evidence.
pages.body: The evidence-to-scale layer distinguishes vision from counted proof and blocks strong public claims until source-verifiable artifacts exist.

sections.title: What changed
sections.text: The repository now has market evidence schemas, pilot outcome dockets, proof velocity metrics, public metrics ledgers, and an evidence room index.

cards.title: Placeholders do not count
cards.text: Examples are visibly labeled and excluded from market evidence scoring.

cards.title: Proof has levels
cards.text: Operator-attested, source-verifiable, and third-party-verified evidence are treated differently.

cards.title: Claims are gated
cards.text: The validator refuses strong scale claims until required evidence categories are present.

sections.title: Evidence categories
sections.text: Product, usage, customer discovery, design partner, pilot, benchmark, commercial, independent validation, fair-value support, and governance evidence are tracked separately.

pages.title: Proof Velocity
pages.headline: Measure how quickly work becomes validated capability.
pages.body: Proof velocity measures the time from mission start to Evidence Docket, validation, settlement, Chronicle entry, and reuse.

sections.title: Not raw activity
sections.text: High proof velocity requires bounded missions, complete evidence, validator review, and reusable capability packages.

sections.title: Tracked outputs
sections.text: The repository tracks missions completed, dockets created, validator reports, release gates, capability packages, reuse events, and review cycle time.

pages.title: Evidence Room
pages.headline: A structured data room for what can be proven.
pages.body: The evidence room indexes artifacts, source provenance, confidentiality, public claim permission, and review status.

sections.title: Public proof, private data
sections.text: Public pages can show aggregates, hashes, and redacted summaries. Sensitive customer, commercial, or legal records stay private unless publication rights are clear.

sections.title: Reviewer path
sections.text: A stakeholder can see what is built, tested, validated, confidential, and still pending without relying on marketing language.

pages.title: Pilot Proof
pages.headline: Every serious pilot ends with a docket.
pages.body: Pilot evidence requires a bounded mission, success criteria, baseline, execution record, validator decision, and follow-up status.

sections.title: Controlled pilots
sections.text: The system is designed for named use cases, pre-agreed success criteria, replayable evidence, and post-pilot acceptance records.

sections.title: Outcome states
sections.text: Pilots are recorded as in progress, completed pending review, validated success, validated mixed, or not validated.

pages.title: Strategic Evidence Scorecard
pages.headline: What can be proven today?
pages.body: The scorecard separates institutional infrastructure readiness from external market proof and recommends the public statement that evidence can carry.

sections.title: Two scores
sections.text: Institutional infrastructure can be strong while market evidence remains pending. That separation protects trust.

sections.title: Strong claim gate
sections.text: The gate requires verified evidence across product, usage, customer, pilot, benchmark, commercial, independent validation, fair-value support, and governance categories.

pages.title: Public Metrics Ledger
pages.headline: Publish the metrics that proof can support.
pages.body: The public metrics ledger exposes proof velocity and evidence completeness while keeping sensitive records private.

sections.title: Safe public reporting
sections.text: The ledger can publish aggregate mission counts, validation counts, reuse rates, and evidence completeness without revealing private customer records.

sections.title: Evidence hashes
sections.text: Private records can be represented by hashes, source classes, and reviewer notes when full disclosure is not appropriate.

pages.title: Independent Validation
pages.headline: Internal proof becomes stronger when reviewed independently.
pages.body: The validation protocol gives external reviewers a Mission Contract, Claims Matrix, Evidence Docket, Risk Ledger, replay notes, and acceptance criteria.

sections.title: Reviewer outputs
sections.text: Validators return a decision, confidence level, evidence gaps, reproduction notes, limitations, and publication recommendation.

sections.title: Trust lift
sections.text: Independent review does not replace judgment, but it improves the evidential quality of serious public claims.

pages.title: Commercial Evidence
pages.headline: Commercial signals are counted carefully and disclosed responsibly.
pages.body: Commercial evidence can include signed records, paid pilots, procurement progression, renewals, and expansion signals, but sensitive details stay private by default.

sections.title: Counted evidence
sections.text: A commercial signal counts only when it has a date, owner, source record, confidentiality level, and public claim permission.

sections.title: Disclosure discipline
sections.text: Names, revenue amounts, contract terms, and legal records are not published unless permission is clear.
